content.text:
*"To everything there is a season, and a time to every 
purpose under the heaven."*{Ecclesiastes 3:1}{If $a$ and $b$ have the same remainder when divided by $n$, they are congruent modulo $n$.}{Carl Friedrich Gauss}

Modular arithmeticā€”"clock arithmetic"ā€”deals with remainders and 
cyclic patterns. It is fundamental to modern cryptography.

content.title: Congruence
content.content:
Let $m$ be a positive integer. Integers $a$ and $b$ are **congruent 
modulo $m$**, written $a \equiv b \pmod{m}$, if $m$ 
divides $a - b$.

Equivalently, $a$ and $b$ have the same remainder when divided by $m$.

content.title: Congruence Properties
content.content:
Let $a, b, c, d$ be integers and $m > 0$. If $a \equiv b \pmod{m}$ and 
$c \equiv d \pmod{m}$, then:

1. $a + c \equiv b + d \pmod{m}$

2. $a - c \equiv b - d \pmod{m}$

3. $ac \equiv bd \pmod{m}$

4. $a^n \equiv b^n \pmod{m}$ for any positive integer $n$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Division in Modular Arithmetic
content.content:
Division does not always work! $6 \equiv 0 \pmod{6}$ and $2 \equiv 2 \pmod{6}$, 
but $6/2 = 3 \not\equiv 0/2 = 0 \pmod{6}$. We can divide by $c$ only if 
$\gcd(c, m) = 1$.

content.title: Computing large powers modulo $m$
content.solution:
Compute successive powers modulo 7:
$$
3^1 &\equiv 3 & 3^2 &\equiv 2 & 3^3 &\equiv 6 \\
3^4 &\equiv 4 & 3^5 &\equiv 5 & 3^6 &\equiv 1 \pmod{7}
$$

Since $3^6 \equiv 1$, and $100 = 6 \cdot 16 + 4$:
\[ 3^{100} = (3^6)^{16} \cdot 3^4 \equiv 1^{16} \cdot 4 = 4 \pmod{7} \]

content.title: Modular Inverse
content.content:
If $\gcd(a, m) = 1$, the **modular inverse** 
of $a$ modulo $m$ is the integer $a^{-1}$ such that:
\[ a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{m} \]

content.title: Fermat's Little Theorem
content.content:
If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then:
\[ a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \]
content.label: Theorem

marginNotes.text:
Let there be lights in the vault of the 
sky... to serve as signs to mark sacred times, and days and years.
